edges.description: Toxic endo-lysosomal storage is reflected pathologically by abnormal autofluorescent storage material in neural tissue.
Toxic endo-lysosomal storage is modeled as part of the inherited lysosomal storage disease process that drives progressive neurodegeneration across NCLs.
Pathogenic CLN-gene disruption of endomembrane and lysosomal processes is modeled upstream of the shared toxic endo-lysosomal storage phenotype.
Disrupted CLN/endolysosomal function has far-ranging cellular effects that include autophagy disruption.
CLN-gene disruption of the endolysosomal network also perturbs synaptic function, providing a route to seizures and myoclonus.
The lysosomal lipopigment storage process is measured pathologically as increased autofluorescent ceroid/lipopigment storage material.
Accumulated lysosomal storage material accompanies the progressive neurodegenerative course of NCL.
Storage disease progression includes retinal degeneration, explaining the prominent visual decline in many NCL subtypes.
Chronic autophagy dysfunction contributes to downstream neuronal degeneration in NCL.
Pathologic apoptotic signaling contributes to downstream neuronal loss and disease progression.
Synaptic dysfunction provides a mechanistic route from NCL cellular pathology to epileptic seizures.
Synaptic dysfunction is modeled as contributing to myoclonus in late-infantile NCL presentations.
Activated or adversely affected glial cells contribute to the progressive neurodegenerative cascade.
The neurodegenerative storage process affects the retina, producing retinal degeneration upstream of visual impairment.
Progressive neurodegeneration contributes to the characteristic loss of vision in NCL.
Ongoing neurodegeneration drives progressive cognitive deterioration.
Progressive neuronal disease contributes to epileptic seizures in NCL.
Childhood-onset neurodegeneration leads to loss of previously acquired developmental function.
Progressive neurodegeneration produces loss of motor function across childhood-onset NCL presentations.
Increased autofluorescent storage material reports the core lysosomal lipopigment accumulation node.

nodes.description: Apoptosis is another recurring component of NCL cellular pathology and likely contributes to neuronal loss.

evidence.snippet: We describe the large variety of experimental models which have aided this research, as well as the most recent technological developments which have shed light on the main mechanisms involved in the cellular pathology, such as apoptosis and autophagy.

nodes.description: NCL tissue pathology includes lysosomal accumulation of abnormal autofluorescent storage material, with ultrastructural fingerprint, curvilinear, and granular osmiophilic deposits in affected tissues.

evidence.reference_title: Clinico-pathological manifestations of variant late infantile neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis (vLINCL) caused by a novel mutation in MFSD8 gene.
evidence.snippet: Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis (NCL) refers to a growing heterogeneous group of neurodegenerative disorders characterized by lysosomal accumulation of abnormal autofluorescent material.

evidence.reference_title: Clinico-pathological manifestations of variant late infantile neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis (vLINCL) caused by a novel mutation in MFSD8 gene.
evidence.snippet: Ultrastructural studies of brain and rectal mucosa disclosed accumulation of storage material in various patterns including fingerprint, curvilinear, and granular osmiophilic deposits consistent with the diagnosis of NCL.

evidence.reference_title: Clinico-pathological manifestations of variant late infantile neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis (vLINCL) caused by a novel mutation in MFSD8 gene.
evidence.snippet: Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis (NCL) refers to a growing heterogeneous group of neurodegenerative disorders characterized by lysosomal accumulation of abnormal autofluorescent material.

readouts.interpretation: Increased autofluorescent storage material reports the core lysosomal lipopigment accumulation node.

nodes.description: Impaired autophagic handling is a recurring component of NCL cellular pathology and contributes to progressive neuronal dysfunction.

evidence.snippet: We describe the large variety of experimental models which have aided this research, as well as the most recent technological developments which have shed light on the main mechanisms involved in the cellular pathology, such as apoptosis and autophagy.

nodes.description: NCL-causing genes encode proteins distributed across the endomembrane system, and convergent dysfunction of this intracellular network is thought to underlie the shared clinical manifestations across subtypes.

evidence.snippet: Ceroid lipofuscinosis neuronal (CLN) genes encode 13 proteins that localize throughout the endomembrane system to regulate a variety of cellular processes.

A growing body of literature supports the networking of CLN genes and proteins within cells, which aligns with the broadly similar cellular and clinical manifestations among the different subtypes of NCL.

nodes.description: Enzyme replacement therapy used in CLN2 disease that slows decline in motor and language function.

evidence.snippet: Treatment with intracerebroventricular cerliponase alfa resulted in slower decline of motor and language functions compared with natural history controls.
Cerliponase alfa is the first therapy for neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 2 that targets the disease etiology.

nodes.description: NCL causes progressive decline in cognition and mental function.

evidence.snippet: Common symptoms of NCLs include the progressive loss of vision, mental and motor deterioration, epileptic seizures, premature death, and, in rare adult-onset cases, dementia.

nodes.description: Progressive deterioration of motor and cognitive function in childhood NCL commonly presents clinically as loss of previously acquired skills.

evidence.snippet: Common symptoms of NCLs include the progressive loss of vision, mental and motor deterioration, epileptic seizures, premature death, and, in rare adult-onset cases, dementia.

nodes.description: Glial cells are affected in NCL and can actively contribute to disease progression, adding a non-neuronal component to the neurodegenerative cascade.

evidence.reference_title: Pathomechanisms in the neuronal ceroid lipofuscinoses.
evidence.snippet: glial cells to be adversely affected and actively contribute to disease progression.

nodes.description: NCL causes progressive loss of motor abilities as part of the psychomotor deterioration seen in childhood-onset disease.

evidence.reference_title: The novel neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis gene MFSD8 encodes a putative lysosomal transporter.
evidence.snippet: including age at onset from 2 to 7 years, epileptic seizures, psychomotor deterioration, myoclonus, loss of vision, and premature death.

nodes.description: Myoclonus is a motor manifestation reported in late-infantile NCL presentations.

evidence.reference_title: The novel neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis gene MFSD8 encodes a putative lysosomal transporter.
evidence.snippet: including age at onset from 2 to 7 years, epileptic seizures, psychomotor deterioration, myoclonus, loss of vision, and premature death.

nodes.description: Mutations in CLN genes ultimately drive a shared neurodegenerative disease process across NCL subtypes.

evidence.snippet: In humans, mutations in CLN genes cause a devastating form of neurodegeneration called neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis (NCL), commonly known as Batten disease.

nodes.description: Progressive retinal degeneration is a tissue-level ocular phenotype that helps explain visual impairment in NCL.

evidence.reference_title: Clinico-pathological manifestations of variant late infantile neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis (vLINCL) caused by a novel mutation in MFSD8 gene.

nodes.description: Epileptic seizures are a frequent clinical manifestation of NCL.

evidence.snippet: Common symptoms of NCLs include the progressive loss of vision, mental and motor deterioration, epileptic seizures, premature death, and, in rare adult-onset cases, dementia.

nodes.description: Endolysosomal disruption in NCL affects synaptic vesicle and synaptic transmission pathways, contributing to epileptic and myoclonic manifestations.

evidence.reference_title: Pathomechanisms in the neuronal ceroid lipofuscinoses.
evidence.snippet: NCL mutations have far-ranging effects on cellular functions including autophagy and synaptic dysfunction.

nodes.description: NCL is defined by toxic endo-lysosomal storage that accompanies the genetically heterogeneous lysosomal defects across the disease group.

evidence.snippet: These are genetic diseases associated with the formation of toxic endo-lysosomal storage.

nodes.description: Progressive loss of vision is a common hallmark phenotype across NCL subtypes.

evidence.snippet: Common symptoms of NCLs include the progressive loss of vision, mental and motor deterioration, epileptic seizures, premature death, and, in rare adult-onset cases, dementia.
